title.zh: 卷七十八 蕭望之傳
title.raw: 卷七十八 蕭望之傳 Volume 78: Xiao Wangzhi

sentences.zh: 卷七十八 蕭望之傳第四十八

translations.literal: Volume 78, Biography of Xiao Wangzhi, Number 48.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 78: Biography of Xiao Wangzhi, the forty-eighth.

sentences.zh: 蕭望之字長倩，東海蘭陵人也，徙杜陵。

translations.literal: Xiao Wangzhi, courtesy name Changqian, was a man of Lanling in Donghai; his family moved to Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao Wangzhi, whose courtesy name was Changqian, came from Lanling in Donghai and later relocated to Duling.

sentences.zh: 家世以田為業，至望之，好學，治《齊詩》，事同縣後倉且十年。

translations.literal: For generations his family had taken farming as their occupation; when it came to Wangzhi, he loved learning, studied the Qi version of the Odes, and served Hou Cang of the same county for nearly ten years.
translations.idiomatic: His people had long lived by the plough; Wangzhi himself loved letters, mastered the Qi recension of the Classic of Odes, and studied under Hou Cang of his county for the better part of a decade.

sentences.zh: 以令詣太常受業，復事同學博士白奇，又從夏侯勝問《論語》、《禮服》。

translations.literal: By order he went to the Grand Master of Ceremonies to receive instruction, then studied again under his fellow student the erudite Bai Qi, and also questioned Xia housheng on the Analects and on Mourning Dress.
translations.idiomatic: On imperial summons he entered the Grand Master of Ceremonies' school, continued under the erudite Bai Qi, a classmate, and took the Analects and Mourning Dress from Xia housheng.

sentences.zh: 京師諸儒稱述焉。

translations.literal: The scholars of the capital praised and recounted him.
translations.idiomatic: The capital's Confucians spoke of him with respect.

sentences.zh: 是時，大將軍霍光秉政，長史丙吉薦儒生王仲翁與望之等數人，皆召見。

translations.literal: At that time the great general Huo Guang held power; his chief clerk Bing Ji recommended the scholar Wang Zhongweng together with Wangzhi and several others, and all were summoned for audience.
translations.idiomatic: While the great general Huo Guang governed, chief clerk Bing Ji nominated the scholar Wang Zhongweng along with Wangzhi and others; they were all called in.

sentences.zh: 先是，左將軍上官桀與蓋主謀殺光，光既誅桀等，後出入自備。

translations.literal: Earlier, the left general Shangguan Jie and the Elder Princess had plotted to kill Guang; after Guang had executed Jie and the rest, thereafter when going out or coming in he guarded himself.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Left General Shangguan Jie and the princess regent had conspired against Huo Guang; once Guang had put them to death, he went about heavily guarded.

sentences.zh: 吏民當見者，露索去刀兵，兩吏挾持。

translations.literal: Officials and commoners who were to have audience were stripped and searched and relieved of knives and weapons, two clerks holding them on either side.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone granted an audience was stripped and searched, disarmed, and marched in flanked by two attendants.

sentences.zh: 望之獨不肯聽，自引出閣曰：「不願見。」

translations.literal: Wangzhi alone refused to obey; he walked out of the gate on his own and said, "I am unwilling to be seen."
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi alone refused; he turned back toward the gate and said, "I decline the audience."

sentences.zh: 吏牽持匈匈。

translations.literal: The clerks pulled and held him with a hubbub.
translations.idiomatic: The guards seized him amid a clamor.

sentences.zh: 光聞之，告吏勿持。

translations.literal: When Guang heard of it, he told the clerks not to hold him.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang heard and ordered them to let him go.

sentences.zh: 望之既至前，說光曰：「將軍以功德輔幼主，將以流大化，致於洽平，是以天下之士延頸企踵，爭願自效，以輔高明。

translations.literal: When Wangzhi had come forward, he remonstrated with Guang, saying, "The general, by merit and virtue, assists the young sovereign and will thereby spread great transformation and bring about peace and order; therefore the empire's gentlemen crane their necks and stand on tiptoe, striving to offer themselves in service to aid your enlightened height.
translations.idiomatic: When Wangzhi at last stood before him, he said, "You support the boy emperor on strength of deed and reputation and mean to carry a great civilizing influence to perfect peace, so men of talent across the realm crane their necks and strain to serve your eminence.

sentences.zh: 今士見者皆先露索挾持，恐非周公相成王躬吐握之禮，致白屋之意。」

translations.literal: Now every gentleman who is granted audience is first stripped, searched, and held on both sides; I fear this is not the ritual by which the Duke of Zhou attended King Cheng, personally spitting out food and interrupting his hair-washing, nor does it convey the intent toward those in plain huts."
translations.idiomatic: Yet every caller is searched and pinioned first. That is hardly the Duke of Zhou's way with the young King Cheng—interrupting meals and baths to see a petitioner—nor does it show the regard due men in rough cottages."

sentences.zh: 於是光獨不除用望之，而仲翁等皆補大將軍史。

translations.literal: Thereupon Guang alone did not appoint Wangzhi to office, while Zhongweng and the rest were all assigned as clerks to the great general.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang therefore passed Wangzhi over while Wang Zhongweng and the others became secretaries on his staff.

sentences.zh: 三歲間，仲翁至光祿大夫、給事中，望之以射策甲科為郎，署小苑東門候。

translations.literal: Within three years Zhongweng reached grand counselor of the palace and attendant within the yellow gates; Wangzhi, by placing first in the archery-and-essay examination in the top grade, became a gentleman and was posted gate warden at the east gate of the small park.
translations.idiomatic: In three years Zhongweng rose to grand counselor of the palace and attendant at the inner gates; Wangzhi, having topped the graded policy exam, was made a gentleman and assigned to the eastern gatehouse of the Lesser Park.

sentences.zh: 仲翁出入從倉頭廬兒，下車趨門，傳呼甚寵，顧謂望之曰：「不肯錄錄，反抱關為？」

translations.literal: Zhongweng went in and out followed by grooms and runners, alighting from his carriage he hurried to the gate, his cries of passage showing great favor; turning to Wangzhi he said, "You would not be docile, and instead embrace the barrier-post?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhongweng traveled with a train of grooms and runners, sprang from his carriage at the gate, and had heralds clear the way in style; glancing at Wangzhi he sneered, "So you would not bend the knee—and now you hug the gate?"

sentences.zh: 望之曰：「各從其志。」

translations.literal: Wangzhi said, "Each follows his own resolve."
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi replied, "Each man follows his own bent."

sentences.zh: 後數年，坐弟犯法，不得宿衛，免歸為郡吏。

translations.literal: Several years later, because his younger brother violated the law, he was not permitted to stand night guard in the palace and was dismissed to return home as a clerk of the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later his brother's crime barred him from palace night duty; he was stripped of rank and sent home as a commandery clerk.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫魏相除望之為屬，察廉為大行治禮丞。

translations.literal: The imperial counselor Wei Xiang appointed Wangzhi as a subordinate; he was inspected as incorrupt and became assistant director for ceremonies in the office of the grand messenger.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Wei Xiang took him on staff; rated incorrupt, he rose to assistant for ritual in the grand messenger's bureau.

sentences.zh: 是時，選博士、諫大夫通政事者補郡國守、相，以望之為平原太守。

translations.literal: At that time those selected as erudites and grandees of remonstrance who were versed in government affairs were assigned to fill the posts of commandery governors and chancellors; Wangzhi was made governor of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: The court was filling governorships and kingdom chancellorships with erudites and remonstrance officials who knew practical administration; Wangzhi received Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 望之雅意在本朝，遠為郡守，內不自得，乃上疏曰：「陛下哀愍百姓，恐德化之不究，悉出諫官以補郡吏，所謂憂其末而忘其本者也。

translations.literal: Wangzhi's true wish was to serve at court; being sent far as a commandery governor, inwardly he was discontented, and therefore he submitted a memorial, saying, "Your Majesty pities the common people, fearing that civilizing virtue may not reach everywhere, and has sent out all the remonstrance officials to supplement the commandery clerks—this is what is called worrying about the twigs and forgetting the root.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi had hoped to remain in the capital; distant appointment as governor sat ill with him. He memorialized: "You pity the people and fear your transforming influence may not reach the ends of the realm, so you have posted every remonstrance officer to the provinces—that is to fret over the branches and forget the root.

sentences.zh: 朝無爭臣則不知過，國無達士則不聞善。

translations.literal: If at court there are no ministers who contend with you, you will not know your faults; if the state has no eminent scholars, good counsel will not be heard.
translations.idiomatic: Without contentious ministers at court you cannot know your errors; without worthy men in the land you will not hear what is right.

sentences.zh: 願陛下選明經術，溫故知新，通於幾微謀慮之士以為內臣，與參政事。

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to select men clear in the classics, able to review the old and know the new, and conversant with subtle planning, to serve as inner ministers and take part in governing affairs.
translations.idiomatic: I ask you to choose men steeped in the classics, who renew their learning from the past, and who grasp fine points of policy, for inner posts and deliberation of state.

sentences.zh: 諸侯聞之，則知國家納諫憂政，亡有闕遺。

translations.literal: When the feudal lords hear of this, they will know that the state welcomes remonstrance and cares for government, and that nothing is neglected.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdoms will then see that the court welcomes criticism and minds its business, with no duty left undone.

sentences.zh: 若此不怠，成、康之道其庶幾乎！

translations.literal: If you do not slacken in this, the way of Cheng and Kang may perhaps be nearly achieved!
translations.idiomatic: Persevere in this, and the age of Kings Cheng and Kang is not beyond reach.

sentences.zh: 外郡不治，豈足憂哉？」

translations.literal: If outer commanderies are not well governed, is that worth worrying about?"
translations.idiomatic: Ill-run border provinces are a lesser worry by comparison."

sentences.zh: 書聞，征入守少府。

translations.literal: When the memorial was received, he was summoned to serve as junior privy treasurer.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the paper and recalled him to be privy treasurer.

sentences.zh: 宣帝察望之經明持重，論議有餘，材任宰相，欲詳試其政事，復以為左馮翊。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan, observing that Wangzhi was clear in the classics, steady in demeanor, ample in debate, and by talent fit to be chancellor, wished to try him thoroughly in administrative affairs, and therefore appointed him left governor of Fengyi.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan saw in him classical learning, gravity, and debating power fit for the chancellorship and meant to test him in government, so he named him left governor of the metropolitan area.

sentences.zh: 望之從少府出為左遷，恐有不合意，即移病。

translations.literal: Wangzhi, going out from junior privy treasurer to the left side, feared it was a demotion for displeasing the ruler, and immediately pleaded illness.
translations.idiomatic: He read the move from privy treasurer to "left" as a demotion for having offended the throne and at once filed a sick note.

sentences.zh: 上聞之，使侍中、成都侯金安上諭意曰：「所用皆更治民以考功。

translations.literal: When the emperor heard of it, he sent the attendant-in-chamber and marquis of Chengdu Jin An'shang to convey his intent, saying, "Those whom I employ are all rotated through governing the people in order to examine merit.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign sent Attendant Jin An'shang of Chengdu to say, "I shift my men into local office to weigh their performance.

sentences.zh: 君前為平原太守日淺，故復試之於三輔，非有所聞也。」

translations.literal: Sir, your time as governor of Pingyuan was short; therefore I am trying you again in the three adjuncts—I have heard nothing against you."
translations.idiomatic: Your tenure in Pingyuan was brief; I am only giving you another tour in the capital region, not implying blame."

sentences.zh: 望之即視事。

translations.literal: Wangzhi thereupon took up his duties.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi then took his seat.

sentences.zh: 是歲，西羌反，漢遣後將軍征之。

translations.literal: That year the Western Qiang rebelled, and Han dispatched the rear general to campaign against them.
translations.idiomatic: That same year the Western Qiang rose; the court sent the rear general against them.

sentences.zh: 京兆尹張敞上書言：「國兵在外，軍以夏發，隴西以北，安定以西，吏民並給轉輸，田事頗廢，素無餘積，雖羌虜以破，來春民食必乏。

translations.literal: The governor of the capital, Zhang Chang, submitted a memorial, saying, "The state's armies are abroad; the troops set out in summer; north of Longxi and west of Anding, officials and commoners alike supply transport; field work is largely abandoned, and ordinarily there are no surplus stores; though the Qiang raiders may be broken, next spring the people's food will surely be lacking.
translations.idiomatic: Governor of the Metropolitan Area Zhang Chang wrote: "With the host in the field since summer, from Longxi north through Anding the people haul supplies; farming is badly disrupted and granaries are thin. Even if the Qiang are crushed, hunger will bite next spring.

sentences.zh: 窮辟之處，買亡所得，縣官谷度不足以振之。

translations.literal: In destitute border regions there is nowhere to buy; the government's grain stores are reckoned insufficient to relieve them.
translations.idiomatic: In those poor marches there is little to buy, and official grain cannot cover relief.

sentences.zh: 願令諸有罪，非盜受財殺人及犯法不得赦者，皆得以差入谷此八郡贖罪。

translations.literal: I beg that all who are guilty of crimes other than theft with violence, accepting bribes, murder, and those who by law may not be pardoned, may in graded fashion pay grain into these eight commanderies to redeem their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Let offenders short of robbery, homicide, unpardonable crimes, and bribery be allowed to pay grain into these eight commanderies in set amounts to commute sentence.

sentences.zh: 務益致谷以豫備百姓之急。」

translations.literal: Strive to increase the bringing in of grain to prepare for the people's urgent need."
translations.idiomatic: That will build reserves against the people's distress."

sentences.zh: 事下有司，望之與少府李強議，以為：「民函明陽之氣，有好義欲利之心，在教化之所助。

translations.literal: The matter was sent to the responsible offices; Wangzhi and the junior privy treasurer Li Qiang deliberated and held that "the people embody the bright yang ether; they have hearts that love righteousness and desire profit; it lies in what transformation teaches.
translations.idiomatic: The case went to the ministries. Wangzhi and Privy Treasurer Li Qiang argued that "the people carry the bright force of yang; they incline to both duty and gain, according to what instruction gives them.

sentences.zh: 堯在上，不能去民欲利之心，而能令其欲利不勝其好義也；

translations.literal: When Yao was above, he could not remove the people's desire for profit, yet could make their desire for profit not overcome their love of righteousness;
translations.idiomatic: Under Yao you could not erase the thirst for gain, yet gain never overcame the love of right;

sentences.zh: 雖桀在上，不能去民好義之心，而能令其好義不勝其欲利也。

translations.literal: even if Jie were above, he could not remove the people's love of righteousness, yet could make their love of righteousness not overcome their desire for profit.
translations.idiomatic: even under Jie you could not stamp out regard for right, yet there gain drowned duty.

sentences.zh: 故堯、桀之分，在於義利而已，道民不可不慎也。

translations.literal: Therefore the distinction between Yao and Jie lies only in righteousness versus profit; in leading the people one cannot be careless.
translations.idiomatic: The gulf between Yao and Jie is simply righteousness against profit—governing the people demands care.

sentences.zh: 今欲令民量粟以贖罪，如此則富者得生，貧者獨死，是貧富異刑而法不一也。

translations.literal: Now if you wish to have the people measure out grain to redeem crimes, in this way the rich will live while the poor alone die—this is different punishment for rich and poor and the law is not one.
translations.idiomatic: To let men buy off sentences with grain saves the wealthy and dooms the poor—two standards of justice.

sentences.zh: 人情，貧窮，父兄囚執，聞出財得以生活，為人子弟者將不顧死亡之患，敗亂之行，以赴財利，求救親戚。

translations.literal: In human feeling, when they are poor and fathers and elder brothers are imprisoned, hearing that paying out wealth can win life, as sons and younger brothers they will not care about the peril of death or the conduct that brings ruin, but will rush toward wealth and profit to save kin.
translations.idiomatic: When kinsmen languish in jail, sons and brothers will court crime and pay any price to ransom them.

sentences.zh: 一人得生，十人以喪，如此，伯夷之行壞，公綽之名滅。

translations.literal: If one man lives, ten perish; in this way the conduct of Boyi is ruined and the reputation of Gongzhuo is extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: One life bought may cost ten; Boyi's standard of integrity and Gongzhuo's repute would collapse.

sentences.zh: 政教一傾，雖有周、召之佐，恐不能復。

translations.literal: Once government and teaching are overturned, even with assistants like the dukes of Zhou and Shao I fear it cannot be restored.
translations.idiomatic: Tilt policy and morals once, and not even Zhou and Shao could set them straight again.

sentences.zh: 古者臧於民，不足則取，有餘則予。

translations.literal: In antiquity the store lay with the people; when there was not enough, it was taken; when there was surplus, it was given.
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity stored wealth in the people—shortfalls were levied, surpluses shared.

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰『爰及矜人，哀此鰥寡』，上惠下也。

translations.literal: The Odes says, "Extend it to the pitiable, pity these widowers and widows"—that is grace from above to below.
translations.idiomatic: The Odes says, "Show pity to the suffering, have mercy on widows and widowers"—that is the ruler's kindness downward.

sentences.zh: 又曰『雨我公田，遂及我私』，下急上也。

translations.literal: It also says, "Rain on our lord's field, then reach to our private plots"—that is urgency from below to above.
translations.idiomatic: It also says, "Let it rain on the public field, then our private plots"—the people's earnest service upward.

sentences.zh: 今有西邊之役，民失作業，雖戶賦口斂以贍其困乏，古之通義，百姓莫以為非。

translations.literal: Now there is campaigning on the western frontier; the people lose their work; though household levies and per-capita exactions supply their distress, by the ancient common principle the commoners do not regard this as wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The western war idles farmers; head taxes and surcharges to relieve them follow ancient precedent and the people accept them.

sentences.zh: 以死救生，恐未可也。

translations.literal: To use death to save life—I fear this may not be permitted.
translations.idiomatic: Trading lives for grain is another matter—I do not think it admissible.

sentences.zh: 陛下布德施教，教化既成，堯、舜亡以加也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty spreads virtue and carries out instruction; transformation is already achieved—even Yao and Shun could not add to it.
translations.idiomatic: Your virtue and teaching have already wrought a transformation Yao and Shun could not surpass.

sentences.zh: 今議開利路以傷既成之化，臣竊痛之。」

translations.literal: Now the proposal would open a path of profit and injure what is already formed in civilization—I privately grieve for it."
translations.idiomatic: To open a profit gate now would scar a finished work—I mourn the suggestion."

sentences.zh: 於是天子復下其議兩府，丞相、御史以難問張敞。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven again sent the proposal down to the two offices; the chancellor and the imperial counselor used it to press Zhang Chang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor bounced the plan to chancellor and counselor; they cross-examined Zhang Chang.

sentences.zh: 敞曰：「少府左馮翊所言，常人之所守耳。

translations.literal: Chang said, "What the junior privy treasurer and the left governor of Fengyi have said is what ordinary men cling to.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chang answered, "The privy treasurer and the left governor speak like men who cling to the letter.

sentences.zh: 昔先帝征四夷，兵行三十餘年，百姓猶不加賦，而軍用給。

translations.literal: In the past the late emperor campaigned against the four barbarians; the armies marched more than thirty years, yet the common people still had no extra taxes levied, and military expenditure was supplied.
translations.idiomatic: The late emperor fought the four quarters for over thirty years without new taxes on the people, and the armies were fed.

sentences.zh: 今羌虜一隅小夷，跳梁於山谷間，漢但令罪人出財減罪以誅之，其名賢於煩擾良民橫興賦斂也。

translations.literal: Now the Qiang bandits are a petty tribe in a corner, hopping about in the valleys; if Han merely has criminals pay out wealth to reduce sentences in order to destroy them, the name of that is worth more than troubling good people and recklessly raising imposts.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang are a trifling hill foe; letting convicts buy commutation to fund their suppression is a cleaner name than harrying taxpayers.

sentences.zh: 又諸盜及殺人犯不道者，百姓所疾苦也，皆不得贖；

translations.literal: Moreover, as for all robbery and murder and those who violate the principle of intolerable conduct—the things the common people bitterly hate—none of these may be ransomed;
translations.idiomatic: Robbery, murder, and intolerable wickedness—the crimes the people hate—would stay ineligible,

sentences.zh: 首匿、見知縱、所不當得為之屬，議者或頗言其法可蠲除，今因此令贖，其便明甚，何化之所亂？

translations.literal: as for harboring fugitives, "see and know" indulgence, and acts that ought not to be done—some debaters have even said their laws might be abolished; now by this order to ransom, the convenience is very clear—where is the confusion of transformation?
translations.idiomatic: while harboring, "see-know" liability, and similar statutes some would repeal would gain a clear outlet. How would civilization suffer?

sentences.zh: 《甫刑》之罰，小過赦，薄罪贖，有金選之品，所從來久矣，何賊之所生？

translations.literal: The punishments of the Fu punishments: small faults are pardoned, light crimes are ransomed, there are grades of metal fines—it has been thus from of old—where would bandits arise?
translations.idiomatic: The "Fu punishments" pardon small slips and redeem light guilt with scaled fines—ancient practice—and breed no more thieves.

sentences.zh: 敞備皁衣二十餘年，嘗聞罪人贖矣，未聞盜賊起也。

translations.literal: I, Chang, have worn the dark robes more than twenty years; I have heard of criminals ransoming—I have not heard of thieves arising because of it.
translations.idiomatic: I have worn court black for twenty years and heard of ransom, not of robbery spawned by it.

sentences.zh: 竊憐涼州被寇，方秋饒時，民尚有饑乏，病死於道路，況至來春將大困乎！

translations.literal: I privately pity Liangzhou, suffering raids; it is the time of autumn plenty, yet the people still have hunger and want, and die of illness on the roads—how much more next spring will they be in great distress!
translations.idiomatic: Liangzhou is ravaged; even in the fat of autumn people starve and die along the road—what of next spring?

sentences.zh: 不早慮所以振救之策，而引常經以難，恐後為重責。

translations.literal: If you do not early consider policies to relieve and save them, but cite the constant classic to oppose this, I fear later you will bear heavy blame.
translations.idiomatic: Fail to plan relief now and hide behind precedent, and you may answer for it harshly later.

sentences.zh: 常人可與守經，未可與權也。

translations.literal: Ordinary men can be made to keep the classic—they cannot be made to exercise discretion.
translations.idiomatic: Mediocre men keep the rulebook; they cannot weigh circumstance.

sentences.zh: 敞幸得備列卿，以輔兩府為職，不敢不盡愚。」

translations.literal: I, Chang, have been fortunate enough to be ranked among the ministers, taking assisting the two offices as my duty—I dare not withhold my foolishness."
translations.idiomatic: I stand among the high ministers charged with aiding both offices—I must speak plainly."

sentences.zh: 望之、強復對曰：「先帝聖德，賢良在位，作憲垂法，為無窮之規，永惟邊竟之不贍，故《金布令甲》曰『邊郡數被兵，離饑寒，夭絕天年，父子相失，令天下共給其費』，固為軍旅卒暴之事也。

translations.literal: Wangzhi and Qiang replied again, saying, "The late emperor's sagely virtue and worthy men in place made statutes and hung down laws, setting a pattern without end; ever mindful that the border regions lacked sufficiency, therefore the Metal-Cloth Ordinance, first series, says, 'Border commanderies have repeatedly suffered arms, parted from cold and hunger, cut off in the prime of life, fathers and sons lost to each other—order the empire jointly to supply their expense'—this was indeed for the sake of military affairs sudden and violent.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi and Li Qiang answered again: "The late emperor's sage example and good men in office wrote laws for the ages, mindful that the frontier could not sustain itself—hence the Metal-Cloth Ordinance, Class A: 'Border counties often see war, hunger, and early death, families torn—let the whole realm share the cost'—meant for sudden military need.

sentences.zh: 聞天漢四年，常使死罪人入五十萬錢減死罪一等，豪強吏民請奪假□，至為盜賊以贖罪。

translations.literal: I have heard that in the fourth year of Tianhan it was customary to have men sentenced to death pay five hundred thousand cash to reduce capital crime one grade; powerful magnates among officials and commoners borrowed and seized on false pretenses (the text is damaged here), to the point of becoming bandits in order to ransom crimes.
translations.idiomatic: We are told that in Tianhan 4 men under death sentence could pay five hundred thousand cash to commute one degree; magnates borrowed, seized, and forged credentials until some turned bandit to raise the fee.

sentences.zh: 其後奸邪橫暴，群盜並起，至攻城邑，殺郡守，充滿山谷，吏不能禁，明詔遣繡衣使者以興兵擊之，誅者過半，然後衰止。

translations.literal: Afterward the wicked and violent ran rampant, bandits rose together, to the point of attacking cities and killing commandery governors, filling the valleys; officials could not stop them; an enlightened edict dispatched embroidered-cloth envoys to raise troops and strike them; more than half were executed before it subsided.
translations.idiomatic: Then crime swelled, robbers massed, stormed towns, slew governors, and clogged the hills until imperial envoys in embroidered robes led troops and killed more than half before order returned.

sentences.zh: 愚以為此使死罪贖之敗也，故曰不便。」

translations.literal: I foolishly hold that this was the failure of letting capital crimes be ransomed; therefore I say it is not expedient."
translations.idiomatic: That, we think, was the fruit of buying off death sentences—hence we call the scheme unwise."

sentences.zh: 時，丞相魏相、御史大夫丙吉亦以為羌虜且破，轉輸略足相給，遂不施敞議。

translations.literal: At the time Chancellor Wei Xiang and Imperial Counselor Bing Ji also held that the Qiang raiders would soon be broken and transport would roughly suffice to supply each other, and therefore did not implement Chang's proposal.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Wei Xiang and Counselor Bing Ji judged the Qiang nearly beaten and supply adequate, so Zhang Chang's plan was dropped.

sentences.zh: 望之為左馮翊三年，京師稱之，遷大鴻臚。

translations.literal: Wangzhi served three years as left governor of Fengyi; the capital praised him, and he was promoted grand herald.
translations.idiomatic: After three years as left governor he won the capital's praise and rose to grand herald.

sentences.zh: 先是，烏孫昆彌翁歸靡因長羅侯常惠上書，願以漢外孫元貴靡為嗣，得復尚少主，結婚內附，畔去匈奴。

translations.literal: Earlier, the Wusun great chanyu Wengguimi, through the marquis of Changluo, Chang Hui, had submitted a memorial, wishing to make his Han-born grandson Yuanguimi heir, to be able again to marry the young princess, bind marriage and attach inward, and turn away from the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier the Wusun ruler Wengguimi had petitioned through Marquis Chang Hui to install his part-Han grandson Yuanguimi and remarry the imperial princess, cleaving to Han and away from the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 詔下公卿議，望之以為：烏孫絕域，信其美言，萬里結婚，非長策也。

translations.literal: An edict was sent down to the dukes and ministers for deliberation; Wangzhi held that Wusun was a land beyond reach; to trust their fine words and bind marriage ten thousand li away was no long-term strategy.
translations.idiomatic: The edict went to council. Wangzhi said Wusun lay at impossible distance; a marriage alliance on mere fine words was no lasting policy.

sentences.zh: 天子不聽。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor would not hear him.

sentences.zh: 神爵二年，遣長羅侯惠使送公主配元貴靡。

translations.literal: In the second year of Shenjue he dispatched the marquis of Changluo, Hui, as envoy to escort the princess to marry Yuanguimi.
translations.idiomatic: In Shenjue 2 he sent Chang Hui to escort the princess to Yuanguimi.

sentences.zh: 未出塞，翁歸靡死，其兄子狂王背約自立。

translations.literal: Before they had left the passes, Wengguimi died; his elder brother's son the Mad King broke the agreement and set himself up.
translations.idiomatic: Before the party crossed the frontier Wengguimi died; a nephew, the "Mad King," broke the pact and seized the throne.

sentences.zh: 惠從塞下上書，願留少主敦煌郡。

translations.literal: Hui, from below the passes, submitted a memorial, begging to leave the young princess at Dunhuang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Chang Hui wrote from the border asking to hold the princess at Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 惠至烏孫，責以負約，因立元貴靡，還迎少主。

translations.literal: When Hui reached Wusun, he reproached them for breaking the agreement, thereupon set up Yuanguimi, and returned to welcome the young princess.
translations.idiomatic: At Wusun he rebuked them for bad faith, enthroned Yuanguimi, and came back for the princess.

sentences.zh: 詔下公卿議，望之復以為：「不可。

translations.literal: An edict was sent down to the dukes and ministers for deliberation; Wangzhi again held that "it cannot be done.
translations.idiomatic: Council was ordered again. Wangzhi still said no.

sentences.zh: 烏孫持兩端，亡堅約，其效可見。

translations.literal: Wusun holds two ends; it has no firm covenant; its effect can be seen.
translations.idiomatic: Wusun plays both sides and keeps no firm pledge—the record shows it.

sentences.zh: 前少主在烏孫四十餘年，恩愛不親密，邊境未以安，此已事之驗也。

translations.literal: Formerly the young princess was in Wusun more than forty years; intimacy and affection were not close; the borderlands were not thereby secure—this is verification from what has already happened.
translations.idiomatic: The last princess dwelt there forty years without real intimacy or a quiet border—that is the precedent.

sentences.zh: 今少主以元貴靡不得立而還，信無負於四夷，此中國之大福也。

translations.literal: Now the young princess returns because Yuanguimi could not be enthroned; in trust there is no letting down the four barbarians—this is a great blessing for the Middle Kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: To bring her home because Yuanguimi failed keeps faith with the outer tribes without loss—a great boon to China.

sentences.zh: 少主不止，繇役將興，其原起此。」

translations.literal: If the young princess does not stop, corvée and campaigns will arise; the source will begin from this."
translations.idiomatic: If she stays, levies and wars will follow—the trouble starts here."

sentences.zh: 天子從其議，征少主還。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven followed his proposal and summoned the young princess back.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed and recalled the princess.

sentences.zh: 後烏孫雖分國兩立，以元貴靡為大昆彌，漢遂不復與結婚。

translations.literal: Later Wusun though split into two states enthroned with Yuanguimi as great chanyu, and Han thereafter did not again bind marriage with them.
translations.idiomatic: Wusun later split into two courts with Yuanguimi as great chanyu, and Han never again married into the line.

sentences.zh: 三年，代丙吉為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the third year he replaced Bing Ji as imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year he succeeded Bing Ji as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 五鳳中匈奴大亂，議者多曰匈奴為害日久，可因其壞亂舉兵滅之。

translations.literal: In the Wufeng period the Xiongnu fell into great disorder; many debaters said the Xiongnu had harmed [China] for a long time and one could take advantage of their ruin and disorder to raise troops and destroy them.
translations.idiomatic: During Wufeng the Xiongnu collapsed into civil strife; many at court urged exploiting the chaos to extirpate them.

sentences.zh: 詔遣中朝大司馬車騎將軍韓增、諸吏富平侯張延壽、光祿勳楊惲、太僕戴長樂問望之計策，望之對曰：「《春秋》惡士□帥師侵齊，聞齊侯卒，引師而還，君子大其不伐喪，以為恩足以服孝子，誼足以動諸侯。

translations.literal: An edict dispatched the grand marshal and chariot-and-cavalry general Han Zeng of the inner court, the various officials and marquis of Fuping Zhang Yanshou, superintendent of the household Yang Yun, and grand coachman Dai Changle to ask Wangzhi for plans; Wangzhi replied, saying, "The Spring and Autumn Annals condemns Shi Hui (the graph in the text is damaged) leading troops to invade Qi; hearing that the marquis of Qi had died, he led the army back; the gentleman praises him for not attacking mourning, holding that kindness is sufficient to subdue filial sons and righteousness sufficient to move the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: The court sent Grand Marshal Han Zeng, Marquis Zhang Yanshou of Fuping, Yang Yun, and Dai Changle to ask his counsel. Wangzhi answered: "The Annals fault Shi Hui for invading Qi with an army; when he learned the marquis had died, he withdrew. The noble man exalts refusing to strike a mourner—grace that wins filial sons and duty that sways the lords.

sentences.zh: 前單于慕化鄉善稱弟，遣使請求和親，海內欣然，夷狄莫不聞。

translations.literal: The former chanyu admired transformation and turned toward goodness, styling himself younger brother, and sent envoys to request peace and kinship; within the seas all were pleased, and the barbarians and Di had none who did not hear.
translations.idiomatic: The last chanyu admired our civilization, called himself younger brother, and sued for peace—all under heaven rejoiced and the steppe heard.

sentences.zh: 未終奉約，不幸為賊臣所殺，今而伐之，是乘亂而幸災也，彼必奔走遠遁。

translations.literal: Before the agreement was fulfilled in service, unluckily he was killed by a wicked minister; now to attack them is to take advantage of disorder and rejoice in disaster; they will surely flee far.
translations.idiomatic: He was murdered by a traitor before the pact ran its course; to strike now would be to profit from calamity—they would scatter beyond reach.

sentences.zh: 不以義動兵，恐勞而無功。

translations.literal: To move troops not by righteousness—I fear labor without achievement.
translations.idiomatic: War without right cause buys toil, not victory.

sentences.zh: 宜遣使者弔問，輔其微弱，救其災患，四夷聞之，咸貴中國之仁義。

translations.literal: It is fitting to dispatch envoys to condole and inquire, assist their weakness, and save their disaster and suffering; when the four barbarians hear of it, all will honor the benevolence and righteousness of the Middle Kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Send envoys to mourn, shore up the weak, and relieve distress—the four quarters will honor Han benevolence.

sentences.zh: 如遂蒙恩得復其位，必稱臣服從，此德之盛也。」

translations.literal: If they then receive favor and are able to restore their positions, they will surely declare themselves subjects and obey—this is the height of virtue."
translations.idiomatic: If grace restores their throne, they will submit in gratitude—the crown of virtue."

sentences.zh: 上從其議，後竟遣兵護輔呼韓邪單于定其國。

translations.literal: The emperor followed his proposal; afterward he did in the end dispatch troops to protect and assist Chanyu Huhanye and settle his state.
translations.idiomatic: The throne took his advice; in time troops did go to shield Huhanye and settle his realm.

sentences.zh: 是時，大司農、中丞耿壽昌奏設常平倉，上善之，望之非壽昌。

translations.literal: At that time the grand minister of agriculture and palace assistant Geng Shouchang memorialized to establish ever-normal granaries; the emperor approved it; Wangzhi opposed Shouchang.
translations.idiomatic: When Grand Minister of Agriculture Geng Shouchang proposed ever-normal granaries, the emperor liked the idea; Wangzhi attacked it.

sentences.zh: 丞相丙吉年老，上重焉，望之又奏言：「百姓或乏困，盜賊未止，二千石多材下不任職。

translations.literal: Chancellor Bing Ji was old; the emperor valued him; Wangzhi again submitted a memorial, saying, "Among the common people some are in want and distress; bandits have not ceased; many of the two-thousand-bushel officials are untalented and low and unfit for their posts.
translations.idiomatic: The aged Chancellor Bing Ji still enjoyed imperial favor, yet Wangzhi reported: "The people still suffer; banditry continues; too many two-thousand-bushel officials lack capacity.

sentences.zh: 三公非其人，則三光為之不明，今首歲日月少光，咎在臣等。」

translations.literal: If the three dukes are not the right men, then the three luminaries for that reason are not bright; now in the first month of the year sun and moon have little light—the fault lies in your servants and others."
translations.idiomatic: When the Three Dukes are wrong men, sun, moon, and stars lose their brightness; this new year they have dimmed—the blame is ours."

sentences.zh: 上以望之意輕丞相，乃下侍中建章衛尉金安上、光祿勳楊惲、御史中丞王忠，並詰問望之。

translations.literal: The emperor, because Wangzhi's meaning slighted the chancellor, thereupon sent down the attendant-in-chamber and commandant of the guards of Jianzhang Jin An'shang, superintendent of the household Yang Yun, and palace secretary Wang Zhong together to interrogate Wangzhi.
translations.idiomatic: Reading this as a snub to the chancellor, the emperor set Jin An'shang, Yang Yun, and Palace Secretary Wang Zhong to question him jointly.

sentences.zh: 望之免冠置對，天子由是不說。

translations.literal: Wangzhi took off his cap and answered; the Son of Heaven for this reason was not pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi doffed his cap and replied; the sovereign took displeasure.

sentences.zh: 後丞相司直繁延壽奏：「侍中謁者良使承製詔望之，望之再拜已。

translations.literal: Later the assistant for affairs to the chancellor Fan Yanshou memorialized, saying, "The attendant-in-chamber and yeoman Liang, acting as envoy, carried the sealed edict to Wangzhi; Wangzhi twice bowed and finished.
translations.idiomatic: Later the chancellor's integrity officer Fan Yanshou charged that when the attendant Liang brought the sealed edict to Wangzhi, Wangzhi finished his double bow,

sentences.zh: 良與望之言，望之不起，因故下手，而謂御史曰『良禮不備』。

translations.literal: When Liang spoke with Wangzhi, Wangzhi did not rise; thereupon he deliberately lowered his hand, and said to the imperial counselor, 'Liang's ritual was not complete.'"
translations.idiomatic: but when Liang addressed him he remained seated and only then lowered his hands, telling the counselor that Liang's etiquette had been incomplete."

sentences.zh: 故事丞相病，明日御史大夫輒問病；

translations.literal: By precedent, when the chancellor fell ill, on the following day the imperial counselor would inquire after his health;
translations.idiomatic: By established usage, if the chancellor took ill the imperial counselor called on him the very next day;

sentences.zh: 朝奏事會庭中，差居丞相後，丞相謝，大夫少進，揖。

translations.literal: when court business was convened in the courtyard, [the counselor] was ranked behind the chancellor; when the chancellor withdrew, the counselor advanced slightly and bowed.
translations.idiomatic: At courtyard sessions he stood behind the chancellor, then stepped forward with a bow when the chancellor stepped back.

sentences.zh: 今丞相數病，望之不問病；

translations.literal: Now the chancellor has often been ill, yet Wangzhi has not inquired after his illness;
translations.idiomatic: The present chancellor had been sick repeatedly, and Wangzhi never asked after him;

sentences.zh: 會庭中，與丞相鈞禮。

translations.literal: when meeting in the courtyard, he observed equal ritual courtesy with the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: In those same sessions he insisted on matching the chancellor gesture for gesture.

sentences.zh: 時議事不合意，望之曰：『侯年寧能父我邪！』

translations.literal: When at times deliberation did not suit his wishes, Wangzhi said, "Can the marquis, by mere seniority, play the father to me?"
translations.idiomatic: When debate went against him he snapped, "Does the old marquis think his years make him my father?"

sentences.zh: 知御史有令不得擅使，望之多使守史自給車馬，之杜陵護視家事。

translations.literal: Knowing that the imperial counselor was under orders not to be employed without authorization, Wangzhi repeatedly had his supervisory clerks supply their own carriages and horses and go to Duling to oversee family affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He knew the counselor was barred from private use of staff, yet he had supervising clerks pay for their own mounts and run errands to Duling for his household.

sentences.zh: 少史冠法冠，為妻先引，又使賣買，私所附益凡十萬三千。

translations.literal: A junior clerk wore the statutory cap, went ahead to lead his wife, and also ran purchases for him; private additions to his wealth totaled 103,000 cash.
translations.idiomatic: A junior clerk wore the law cap, led his wife about, and brokered deals for him, padding private accounts by 103,000 cash.

sentences.zh: 案望之大臣，通經術，居九卿之右，本朝所仰，至不奉法自修，踞慢不遜攘，受所監臧二百五十以上，請逮捕系治。」

translations.literal: Consider Wangzhi: a great minister, versed in the classics, seated above the nine ministers, on whom the court relies—to reach the point of refusing to keep the law or discipline himself, of insolence and disrespect, and of taking more than 250 cash in illicit gains from those he oversaw: we request his arrest and trial."
translations.idiomatic: He was a pillar of state, steeped in the classics and ranked above the nine ministers, yet he flouted the law, sneered at propriety, and pocketed over 250 cash from subordinates—arrest and trial are in order."

sentences.zh: 上於是策望之曰：「有司奏君責使者禮，遇丞相亡禮，廉聲不聞，敖慢不遜，亡以扶政，帥先百僚。

translations.literal: The emperor then issued a rescript to Wangzhi, saying, "The offices charge you, sir, with faulting envoys' ritual, treating the chancellor without courtesy, leaving no reputation for integrity, arrogance and defiance—unable to uphold government or lead the hundred officials.
translations.idiomatic: The throne answered him: "Officials report that you slighted imperial envoys, insulted the chancellor, showed no reputation for integrity, and swaggered in a way no leader of the bureaucracy should.

sentences.zh: 君不深思，陷於茲穢，朕不忍致君於理，使光祿勳惲策詔，左遷君為太子太傅，授印。

translations.literal: Sir, you did not think deeply and fell into this disgrace; We are reluctant to hand you, sir, to the judges, and therefore send Superintendent of the Household Yang Yun with a rescript, demoting you, sir, to grand tutor to the heir apparent, and conferring a seal.
translations.idiomatic: You fell into this stain through carelessness; We will not drag you before a tribunal but send Yang Yun with edict and seal, demoting you to tutor of the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 其上故印使者，便道之官。

translations.literal: Deliver your former seal to the envoy and proceed by the shortest route to your post.
translations.idiomatic: Return your old seal to the courier and take the direct road to your new duty.

sentences.zh: 君其秉道明孝，正直是與，帥意亡愆，靡有後言。」

translations.literal: Sir, uphold the way, make filial piety clear, cleave to the upright, guide your mind without fault, and give Us no cause for second thoughts."
translations.idiomatic: Hold fast to the Way, teach true filial duty, stand with the righteous, govern your temper, and do not make Us regret this mercy."

sentences.zh: 望之既左遷，而黃霸代為御史大夫。

translations.literal: After Wangzhi was demoted, Huang Ba replaced him as imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi's demotion opened the path for Huang Ba as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 數月間，丙吉薨，霸為丞相。

translations.literal: Within a few months Bing Ji died; Ba became chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji died within months, and Ba stepped up to the chancellorship.

sentences.zh: 霸薨，於定國復代焉。

translations.literal: When Ba died, Yu Dingguo succeeded him in turn.
translations.idiomatic: When Ba in turn died, Yu Dingguo inherited the post.

sentences.zh: 望之遂見廢，不得相。

translations.literal: Wangzhi was thus set aside and never became chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi had been passed over; the chancellorship never came to him.

sentences.zh: 為太傅，以《論語》、《禮服》授皇太子。

translations.literal: As grand tutor he instructed the heir apparent in the Analects and Mourning Dress.
translations.idiomatic: As grand tutor he lectured the crown prince on the Analects and the Mourning Dress classic.

sentences.zh: 初，匈奴呼韓邪單于來朝，詔公卿議其儀，丞相霸、御史大夫定國議曰：「聖王之制，施德行禮，先京師而後諸夏，先諸夏而後夷狄。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Chanyu Huhanye of the Xiongnu came to court, an edict ordered the dukes and ministers to debate his protocol; Chancellor Ba and Imperial Counselor Dingguo proposed, saying, "Under the sage kings' institutions one spreads virtue and performs ritual: first the capital, then the central domains; first the central domains, then the Yi and Di.
translations.idiomatic: When Huhanye first visited court, the council debated protocol. Chancellor Huang Ba and Counselor Yu Dingguo argued that sage kings ordered civilization from the capital outward—heartland first, then the barbarians.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『率禮不越，遂視既發；

translations.literal: The Odes says, "Observing ritual without overstepping, then looking—already issued;
translations.idiomatic: The Odes read, "He kept every rite within its bounds, then turned his gaze abroad;

sentences.zh: 相士烈烈，海外有截。』

translations.literal: the host of warriors is fierce; beyond the seas they are brought to heel."
translations.idiomatic: his hosts blazed with discipline, and even beyond the seas rivals were cut down."

sentences.zh: 陛下聖德充塞天地，光被四表，匈奴單于鄉風慕化，奉珍朝賀，自古未之有也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty's sagely virtue fills heaven and earth and shines to the four quarters; the Xiongnu chanyu has turned on the wind and yearned for civilization, offering tribute and congratulations at court—such a thing has never occurred since antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Your virtue fills heaven and earth; the chanyu has bent to the breeze of civilization with gifts and homage—unprecedented in the records.

sentences.zh: 其禮儀宜如諸侯王，位次在下。」

translations.literal: His ceremony should match that of the kings of the feudal domains, his place in the order below theirs."
translations.idiomatic: He should therefore be received like a feudal king, but seated below the Han princes."

sentences.zh: 望之以為：「單于非正朔所加，故稱敵國，宜待以不臣之禮，位在諸侯王上。

translations.literal: Wangzhi held that "the chanyu is not one on whom the reigning calendar is imposed; therefore he is termed a rival power and should be received with the etiquette due one who is not made a subject—his seat above the kings of the domains.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi countered that the chanyu stood outside the calendar of the throne and counted as a peer state; he deserved guest rites, not subject rites, and a seat above the imperial clansmen.

sentences.zh: 外夷稽首稱籓，中國讓而不臣，此則羈縻之誼，謙亨之福也。

translations.literal: The outer tribes kowtow and call themselves vassals; China yields and does not treat them as subjects—this is the principle of loose reins, the blessing of yielding prosperity.
translations.idiomatic: They may kowtow and call themselves vassals while China graciously withholds the language of mastery—that is the old policy of loose reins and the fortune of modest success.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰『戎狄荒服』，言其來服，荒忽亡常。

translations.literal: The Documents says, "Rong and Di render wild service," meaning they come to serve, fleeting and inconstant.
translations.idiomatic: The Documents speaks of the Rong and Di in "wild service"—they attend fitfully and without fixed duty.

sentences.zh: 如使匈奴後嗣卒有鳥竄鼠伏，闕如朝享，不為畔臣。

translations.literal: If later Xiongnu heirs should end up scattering like birds or hiding like rats, omitting court audiences, they will not count as rebel vassals.
translations.idiomatic: Should their descendants someday skulk away and skip court, they still would not be treated as traitor-subjects.

sentences.zh: 信讓行乎蠻貉，福祚流於亡窮，萬世之長策也。」

translations.literal: Trust and deference extended to the Man and Mo, blessings flowing without end—this is a strategy for ten thousand generations."
translations.idiomatic: Patience and courtesy toward the northern tribes would spread blessing without end—the true long-term design."

sentences.zh: 天子采之，下詔曰：「蓋聞五帝、三王教化所不施，不及以政。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven adopted it and issued an edict, saying, "We have heard that where the Five Thearchs and Three Kings did not extend transformation, government did not reach.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took his advice and proclaimed: "Where the Five Thearchs and Three Kings did not teach, law could not reach.

sentences.zh: 今匈奴單于稱北籓，朝正朔，朕之不逮，德不能弘覆。

translations.literal: Now the Xiongnu chanyu styles himself a northern vassal and attends Our calendar; We are unequal to the task—Our virtue cannot shelter all.
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu now calls himself a northern bulwark and accepts Our calendar, yet Our virtue is too slight to embrace every people.

sentences.zh: 其以客禮待之，令單于位在諸侯王上，贊謁稱臣而不名。」

translations.literal: Treat him with guest ritual; order the chanyu's seat above the kings of the domains, and in audience announcements style him 'subject' without speaking his name."
translations.idiomatic: Receive him as an honored guest, seat him above the kings, and in the levee let heralds call him 'subject' without intoning his personal name."

sentences.zh: 初，宣帝不甚從儒術，任用法律，而中書宦官用事。

translations.literal: Earlier, Emperor Xuan did not much heed Confucian learning but relied on statute, while eunuchs of the palace secretariat wielded power.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan the court favored law over Confucian rhetoric, and eunuch directors of the secretariat held real leverage.

sentences.zh: 中書令弘恭、石顯久典樞機，明習文法，亦與車騎將軍高為表裡，論議常獨持故事，不從望之等。

translations.literal: Palace secretariat directors Hong Gong and Shi Xian long controlled the levers of government, were expert in written law, and also worked hand in glove with chariot-and-cavalry general Wang Gao; in debate they routinely insisted on precedent alone and would not follow Wangzhi and his allies.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Gong and Shi Xian had long run the machinery of state, knew every clause of the code, and moved in lockstep with General Wang Gao, stonewalling Wangzhi with citations of old precedent.

sentences.zh: 恭、顯又時傾仄見詘。

translations.literal: Gong and Xian were also at times overborne and rebuffed.
translations.idiomatic: The pair were themselves sometimes cornered and refuted in debate.

sentences.zh: 望之以為中書政本，宜以賢明之選，自武帝游宴後庭，故用宦者，非國舊制，又違古不近刑人之義，白欲更置士人，由是大與高、恭、顯忤。

translations.literal: Wangzhi held that the secretariat was the root of administration and ought to be staffed with worthy, enlightened men; since Emperor Wu had roamed and feasted in the inner palace, eunuchs had been used—it was not the dynasty's original system and violated the ancient principle of not keeping mutilated men close; he proposed replacing them with literati, and for this reason deeply opposed Gao, Gong, and Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi argued that the secretariat was the administrative heart and should be manned by scholars; since Emperor Wu's harem diversions, castrates had filled those desks—neither ancient practice nor wise policy—and he pressed to put gentlemen in their place, making bitter enemies of Gao, Gong, and Xian.

sentences.zh: 上初即位，謙讓重改作，議久不定，出劉更生為宗正。

translations.literal: When the emperor had first ascended the throne, he was modest and hesitant over sweeping reform; deliberation dragged on unsettled, and Liu Gengsheng was sent out as director of the imperial clan.
translations.idiomatic: The young emperor shrank from sweeping reform; while debate stalled, Liu Gengsheng was parked in the Directorate of Imperial Clan Affairs.

sentences.zh: 望之、堪數薦名儒茂才以備諫官。

translations.literal: Wangzhi and Zhou Kan repeatedly recommended noted scholars and flourishing talents to fill remonstrance posts.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi and Zhou Kan kept nominating distinguished scholars for remonstrance duties.

sentences.zh: 會稽鄭朋陰欲附望之，上疏言車騎將軍高遣客為奸利郡國，及言許、史子弟罪過。

translations.literal: Zheng Peng of Kuaiji covertly wished to attach himself to Wangzhi; he submitted a memorial charging that chariot-and-cavalry general Wang Gao had sent agents to pursue illicit profit in the commanderies and kingdoms, and also detailing faults of the Xu and Shi younger kin.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Peng of Kuaiji curried favor by memorializing against General Wang Gao's clients for corruption in the provinces and against the Xu and Shi clans.

sentences.zh: 章視周堪，堪白令朋待詔金馬門。

translations.literal: He showed the draft to Zhou Kan; Kan reported it and had Peng await edicts at the Golden Horse Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Kan read the paper, forwarded it, and had Peng posted as an expectant scholar at the Golden Horse Gate.

sentences.zh: 朋奏記望之曰：「將軍體周、召之德，秉公綽之質，有卞莊之威。

translations.literal: Peng addressed Wangzhi in a note, saying, "You, general, embody the virtue of the dukes of Zhou and Shao, bear the substance of Meng Gongchuo, and command the awe of Bian Zhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Peng wrote flatteringly that the general combined the virtue of the Zhou dukes, the steadiness of Meng Gongchuo, and the fierceness of Bian Zhuang.

sentences.zh: 至乎耳順之年，履折衝之位，號至將軍，誠士之高致也。

translations.literal: Having reached the 'obedient ear' years, treading the post that turns back assaults, titled up to general—truly the high mark of a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: At sixty he held the rank that repels enemies—a pinnacle few scholars reach.

sentences.zh: 窟穴黎庶莫不歡喜，咸曰將軍其人也。

translations.literal: The common folk in their dens and caves all rejoice, saying the general is such a man.
translations.idiomatic: Commoners in every lane hailed him as the man of the hour.

sentences.zh: 今將軍規□雲若管、晏而休，遂行日仄至周、召乃留乎？

translations.literal: Now does the general intend to take the measure of clouds like Guan Zhong and Yan Ying and then rest, or press on through the slanting sun until the dukes of Zhou and Shao detain you? (The manuscript is damaged at one graph.)
translations.idiomatic: Would he, like Guan and Yan, retire at the height of his name, or drive himself past sunset until the Zhou dukes themselves called him back? (One character in the text is missing.)

sentences.zh: 若管、晏而休，則下走將歸延陵之皋，修農圃之疇，畜雞種黍，俟見二子，沒齒而已矣。

translations.literal: If you rest like Guan and Yan, then your humble servant will retire to the heights of Yanling, tend the plots of his farm, raise chickens and plant millet, await a meeting with the two masters, and with that end his days.
translations.idiomatic: If he chose the first path, Peng swore, he would withdraw to Yanling, plough his fields, and live out his days in obscurity.

sentences.zh: 如將軍昭然度行，積思塞邪枉之險蹊，宣中庸之常政，興周、召之遺業，親日仄之兼聽，則下走其庶幾願竭區區，底厲鋒鍔，奉萬分之一。」

translations.literal: If the general clearly gauges conduct, amasses thought to block the treacherous byways of wrong, spreads the constant government of the Mean, revives the legacy of Zhou and Shao, and personally heeds comprehensive counsel at the day's end, then perhaps your humble servant might exhaust his petty strength, hone his edge, and offer one ten-thousandth."
translations.idiomatic: If instead the general opened straight paths, sealed off crooked schemes, enacted the steady policy of the Mean, revived the Zhou dukes' work, and listened wide and late, Peng would sharpen every talent and give the thousandth part of what he could."

sentences.zh: 望之見納朋，接待以意。

translations.literal: Wangzhi, having accepted Peng, received him according to inclination.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi took the bait and treated Peng with cordial favor.

sentences.zh: 朋數稱述望之，短車騎將軍，言許、史過失。

translations.literal: Peng repeatedly praised Wangzhi, disparaged the chariot-and-cavalry general, and cited Xu and Shi misconduct.
translations.idiomatic: Peng returned the favor by praising Wangzhi, attacking General Wang Gao, and airing Xu and Shi scandals.

sentences.zh: 後數月，制詔御史：「國之將興，尊師而重傅。

translations.literal: Several months later an edict to the imperial counselor: "When a state is about to flourish, it honors its teacher and esteems its tutor.
translations.idiomatic: Months later the emperor told the counselor: "A rising state exalts its teachers.

sentences.zh: 故前將軍望之傅朕八年，道以經術，厥功茂焉。

translations.literal: Therefore the former general Wangzhi tutored Us eight years, guiding Us with the classics; his merit was abundant.
translations.idiomatic: The former general Wangzhi instructed Us in the classics for eight years with conspicuous merit.

sentences.zh: 其賜望之爵關內侯，食邑六百戶，給事中，朝朔望，坐次將軍」天子方倚欲以為丞相，會望之子散騎中郎人及上書訟望之前事，事下有司，復奏：「望之前所坐明白，無譖訴者，而教子上書，稱引亡辜之《詩》，失大臣體，不敬，請逮捕。」

translations.literal: Let him be granted rank of marquis within the passes, sustenance of six hundred households, attendant within the yellow gates, audience at the new and full moons, seating next below the generals." The Son of Heaven was on the point of relying on him to make him chancellor, when Wangzhi's son Ren, gentleman rider at large, submitted a memorial disputing Wangzhi's earlier case; the matter went to the offices, which reported again: "What Wangzhi was earlier charged with was clear; there was no false accuser; yet he taught his son to memorialize, citing an Ode that speaks of innocence—he lost the bearing of a great minister; it is irreverent; we request arrest."
translations.idiomatic: He awarded Wangzhi marquis-within-the-passes rank, six hundred households, palace attendant status, audiences on the new and full moons, and a seat just below the generals." The emperor had meant to raise him to chancellor, but his son Ren, a gentleman rider, petitioned to reopen an old case; the ministries replied that the earlier verdict was sound, that no one had slandered him, yet teaching a son to quote the Odes on innocence was unworthy of a great minister—disrespect—and asked for his arrest.

sentences.zh: 弘恭、石顯等知望之素高節，不詘辱，建白：「望之前為將軍輔政，欲排退許、史，專權擅朝。

translations.literal: Hong Gong, Shi Xian, and others knew Wangzhi had always borne lofty integrity and would not bend to humiliation; they memorialized: "Wangzhi formerly as general aided the government, wishing to push aside Xu and Shi and monopolize the court.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Gong and Shi Xian, knowing Wangzhi would die before disgrace, wrote that as chief minister he had tried to oust the Xu and Shi factions and dominate the government.

sentences.zh: 幸得不坐，復賜爵邑，與聞政事，不悔過服罪，深懷怨望，教子上書，歸非於上，自以托師傅，懷終不坐。

translations.literal: Fortunate enough to escape conviction, again granted rank and fief, participating in government—yet he has not repented or accepted guilt, harbors deep resentment, taught his son to memorialize, attributing fault to the sovereign, trusting on his status as tutor, expecting never to be convicted.
translations.idiomatic: Spared once, enriched again, and still in council, he showed no remorse, nursed a grudge, coached his son to blame the throne, and assumed the tutor's mantle would shield him forever.

sentences.zh: 非頗詘望之於牢獄，塞其怏怏心，則聖朝亡以施恩厚。」

translations.literal: Unless Wangzhi is somewhat broken into prison to stop his sullen heart, the sagely court will have no way to show generous favor."
translations.idiomatic: Only by humbling him behind bars could the court stifle his sulk and still show mercy."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「蕭太傅素剛，安肯就吏？」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Grand Tutor Xiao has always been rigid; how would he consent to go to the clerks?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor protested, "The grand tutor is proud—he will never submit to petty clerks."

sentences.zh: 顯等曰：「人命至重，望之所坐，語言薄罪，必亡所憂。」

translations.literal: Xian and others said, "Human life is weightiest; what Wangzhi is charged with is a light offense of words—he is sure to have no worry."
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian answered that lives were precious, the new charge a verbal slight, and Wangzhi would have nothing to fear.

sentences.zh: 上乃可其奏。

translations.literal: The emperor then approved their memorial.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor relented and approved the arrest.

sentences.zh: 顯等封以付謁者，敕令召望之手付，因令太常急發執金吾車騎馳圍其第。

translations.literal: Xian and others sealed the document and handed it to the yeoman, ordering him to summon Wangzhi and deliver it by hand, and also ordered the Grand Master of Ceremonies to hurry dispatch the metropolitan commandant's chariot and cavalry troops to gallop and surround his residence.
translations.idiomatic: They sealed the order, told the usher to hand it to Wangzhi in person, and had the Grand Master of Ceremonies rouse the metropolitan commandant to surround his house with horse and chariot.

sentences.zh: 使者至，召望之。

translations.literal: When the envoy arrived, he summoned Wangzhi.
translations.idiomatic: The messenger came and summoned him.

sentences.zh: 望之欲自殺，其夫人止之，以為非天子意。

translations.literal: Wangzhi wished to kill himself; his wife stopped him, thinking it was not the Son of Heaven's intent.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi reached for poison; his wife restrained him, sure the emperor could not mean it.

sentences.zh: 望之以問門下生硃雲。

translations.literal: Wangzhi asked his student Zhu Yun about it.
translations.idiomatic: He turned to his disciple Zhu Yun for counsel.

sentences.zh: 雲者好節士，勸望之自裁。

translations.literal: Yun was one who admired men of integrity; he urged Wangzhi to take his own life.
translations.idiomatic: Yun, a man who honored uncompromising heroes, urged suicide.

sentences.zh: 於是望之仰天歎曰：「吾嘗備位將相，年逾六十矣，老入牢獄，苟求生活，不亦鄙乎！」

translations.literal: Thereupon Wangzhi, sighing toward heaven, said, "I have filled the posts of general and minister; my years exceed sixty; to enter old age in prison and cravenly cling to life—would it not be base!"
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi looked skyward and cried, "I have worn general's and minister's ribbons; I am past sixty—would I crawl through a jail for a few more breaths?"

sentences.zh: 字謂雲曰：「游，趣和藥來，無久留我死！」

translations.literal: He addressed him by style, saying to Yun, "You—hurry and bring the mixed poison; do not long delay my death!"
translations.idiomatic: Calling him by his style, "You," he said, "fetch the draught at once—do not make me wait in shame!"

sentences.zh: 竟飲鴆自殺。

translations.literal: In the end he drank poison and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: He drank the poison and died.

sentences.zh: 天子聞之驚，拊手曰：「曩固疑其不就牢獄，果然殺吾賢傅！」

translations.literal: When the Son of Heaven heard it he was startled; clapping his hands he said, "Earlier We indeed doubted he would not submit to prison; in the end he has killed Our worthy tutor!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor struck his palm and groaned, "We feared he would never endure a cell—he has murdered Our finest teacher!"

sentences.zh: 是時，太官方上晝食，上乃卻食，為之涕泣，哀慟左右。

translations.literal: At that time the palace kitchen had just served the noon meal; the emperor then pushed aside his food, wept for him, and grieved until those at his left and right were stricken.
translations.idiomatic: The noon meal had just arrived; he pushed it away and wept until his attendants wept with him.

sentences.zh: 於是召顯等責問以議不詳。

translations.literal: Thereupon he summoned Xian and others and rebuked them for the memorial not having been thorough.
translations.idiomatic: He called in Shi Xian and his party and scolded them for the careless memorial.

sentences.zh: 皆免冠謝，良久然後已。

translations.literal: All took off their caps and apologized; only after a long time did it stop.
translations.idiomatic: They doffed their caps and begged forgiveness until the storm slowly passed.

sentences.zh: 望之有罪死，有司請絕其爵邑。

translations.literal: Wangzhi having died under accusation, the offices requested to strip his noble rank and fief.
translations.idiomatic: Because he had died a condemned man, the ministries asked to revoke his title and fief.

sentences.zh: 有詔加恩，長子人及嗣為關內侯。

translations.literal: There was an edict showing added grace: his eldest son Ren and heirs were made marquises within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: An edict tempered justice: his eldest son Ren and the heir line kept marquis-within-the-passes status.

sentences.zh: 天子追念望之，不忘每歲時遣使者祠祭望之塚，終元帝世。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven thought back on Wangzhi; each year he did not fail to send envoys to sacrifice at Wangzhi's tomb—for the whole of Emperor Yuan's reign.
translations.idiomatic: For the rest of Emperor Yuan's reign the court sent offerings to his tomb every season.

sentences.zh: 望之八子，至大官者育、咸、由。

translations.literal: Wangzhi had eight sons; those who reached high office were Yu, Xian, and You.
translations.idiomatic: Of his eight sons, Yu, Xian, and You rose highest.

sentences.zh: 哀帝時，南郡江中多盜賊，拜育為南郡太守。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Ai there were many bandits on the Yangtze in Nan commandery; Yu was appointed governor of Nan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ai the middle Yangtze swarmed with pirates, and Yu was named governor of Nan commandery.

sentences.zh: 上以育耆舊名臣，乃以三公使車載育入殿中受策，曰：「南郡盜賊群輩為害，朕甚憂之。

translations.literal: The emperor, because Yu was a venerable old famous minister, had him brought in the three dukes' carriage into the hall to receive his commission, saying, "Nan commandery's bandit bands do harm; We are greatly worried.
translations.idiomatic: Treating him as a senior statesman, the emperor sent a ducal carriage to fetch him into the hall and said, "The river bandits plague Nan commandery, and the matter weighs on Us.

sentences.zh: 以太守威信素著，故委南郡太守，之官，其於為民除害，安元元而已，亡拘於小文。」

translations.literal: Because the governor's awe-inspiring trust is long established, We therefore entrust you with Nan commandery; go to your post, and in removing harm for the people and settling the common folk alone, do not be bound by petty regulations."
translations.idiomatic: Your name alone commands fear; take the post and rid the people of evil without fussing over minor statutes."

sentences.zh: 加賜黃金二十斤。

translations.literal: In addition he was granted twenty jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: The court added a gift of twenty jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 育至南郡，盜賊靜。

translations.literal: When Yu reached Nan commandery, the bandits grew quiet.
translations.idiomatic: When Yu entered Nan commandery, the river grew still.

sentences.zh: 病去官，起家復為光祿大夫執金吾，以壽終於官。

translations.literal: Ill, he left office; from commoner status he was again summoned as grand counselor of the palace and metropolitan commandant, and died in office at a full span of years.
translations.idiomatic: Illness forced him out, but he was recalled from private life as grand counselor of the palace and metropolitan commandant and died in harness at a ripe age.

sentences.zh: 育為人嚴猛尚威，居官數免，稀遷。

translations.literal: Yu by nature was stern, fierce, and honored awe; in office he was several times dismissed, rarely promoted.
translations.idiomatic: Yu was harsh, fond of intimidation, often cashiered, seldom promoted.

sentences.zh: 少與陳咸、硃博為友，著聞當世。

translations.literal: In youth he was friends with Chen Xian and Zhu Bo, famed in his generation.
translations.idiomatic: In youth he befriended Chen Xian and Zhu Bo and was celebrated in his day.

sentences.zh: 往者有王陽、貢公，故長安語曰「蕭、硃結綬，王、貢彈冠」，言其相薦達也。

translations.literal: Formerly there were Wang Yang and Gong Yu, hence the Chang'an saying, "When Xiao and Zhu tie the seal ribbons, Wang and Gong adjust their caps"—speaking of recommending one another.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the old capital rhyme: "When Xiao and Zhu don seals, Wang and Gong dust off their caps"—meaning mutual promotion.

sentences.zh: 始育與陳咸俱以公卿子顯名，咸最先進，年十八，為左曹，二十餘，御史中丞。

translations.literal: At first Yu and Chen Xian both were famed as sons of dukes and ministers; Xian rose first, at eighteen becoming left aide, in his twenties imperial palace secretary.
translations.idiomatic: Yu and Chen Xian, both sons of the elite, rose early—Xian at eighteen was a left aide and in his twenties palace secretary.

sentences.zh: 時，硃博尚為杜陵亭長，為咸、育所攀援，入王氏。

translations.literal: At that time Zhu Bo was still a village headman of Duling; Xian and Yu pulled him up, and he entered the Wang clan's circle.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Bo was still a Duling precinct chief when Xian and Yu pulled him into the Wang faction.

sentences.zh: 後遂並歷刺史、郡守相，及為九卿，而博先至將軍上卿，歷位多於咸、育，遂至丞相。

translations.literal: Later all three served as regional inspectors, governors, and kingdom chancellors, and reached the nine ministers; Bo first attained general and senior minister, holding more posts than Xian and Yu, and finally became chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: All three later governed provinces and sat among the nine ministers; Bo climbed first to general and senior minister, outstripped the other two in offices, and ended as chancellor.

sentences.zh: 育與博後有隙，不能終，故世以交為難。

translations.literal: Yu and Bo later fell out and could not remain friends; therefore the world esteems lasting friendship hard.
translations.idiomatic: Yu and Bo later split, which is why men say friendship at the top is perilous.

sentences.zh: 咸字仲君，為丞相史，舉茂材，好畤令，遷淮陽、泗水內史，張掖、弘農、河東太守。

translations.literal: Xian, courtesy name Zhongjun, was a chancellor's clerk, recommended as flourishing talent, magistrate of Haozhi, promoted to inner steward of Huaiyang and Sishui, and governor of Zhangye, Hongnong, and Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Xian, style Zhongjun, rose from chancellor's clerk through "flourishing talent" nomination to magistrate of Haozhi, then inner steward of Huaiyang and Sishui, then governor of Zhangye, Hongnong, and Hedong.

sentences.zh: 所居有跡，數增秩賜金。

translations.literal: Wherever he served he left a record; repeatedly his salary rank was raised and gold granted.
translations.idiomatic: Every post showed results; the court repeatedly raised his rank and showered him with gold.

sentences.zh: 後免官，復為越騎校尉、護軍都尉、中郎將，使匈奴，至大司農，終官。

translations.literal: Later dismissed, he again became colonel of the swift riders, commandant of guards, general of the gentlemen, envoy to the Xiongnu, rose to grand minister of agriculture, and ended his career in office.
translations.idiomatic: After a dismissal he returned as swift-riders colonel, guards commandant, general of the gentlemen, envoy to the Xiongnu, and finally grand minister of agriculture, dying in service.

sentences.zh: 由字子驕，為丞相西曹衛將軍掾，遷謁者，使匈奴副校尉。

translations.literal: You, courtesy name Ziqiao, was western bureau clerk to the chancellor and aide to the guard general, promoted to yeoman, deputy colonel on mission to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: You, style Ziqiao, served the chancellor's western bureau and the guard general as aide, then yeoman and deputy colonel to the Xiongnu mission.

sentences.zh: 後舉賢良，為定陶令，遷太原都尉，安定太守。

translations.literal: Later recommended as worthy and good, he became magistrate of Dingtao, promoted to chief commandant of Taiyuan, governor of Anding.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as worthy and good, he governed Dingtao, then Taiyuan as chief commandant, then Anding as governor.

sentences.zh: 治郡有聲，多稱薦者。

translations.literal: His commandery government had a reputation; many praised and recommended him.
translations.idiomatic: His administration drew praise and steady nominations.

sentences.zh: 初，哀帝為定陶王時，由為定陶令，失王指，頃之，制書免由為庶人。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Emperor Ai was prince of Dingtao, You was magistrate of Dingtao; he missed the prince's intent; before long an edict dismissed You to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: While Emperor Ai was still prince of Dingtao, You was its magistrate and lost the prince's favor; an edict soon reduced him to commoner.

sentences.zh: 哀帝崩，為復土校尉、京輔左輔都尉，遷江夏太守。

translations.literal: After Emperor Ai died, he became colonel of the burial mounds, chief commandant of the capital's left flank, and was promoted governor of Jiangxia.
translations.idiomatic: When Ai died, You rose as colonel of the burial mounds, then chief commandant of the capital left, then governor of Jiangxia.

sentences.zh: 平江賊成重等有功，增秩為陳留太守，元始中，作明堂辟雍，大朝諸侯，征由為大鴻臚，會病，不及賓贊，還歸故官，病免。

translations.literal: For pacifying the Jiang bandits Cheng Zhong and others he was raised in rank to governor of Chenliu; in the Yuanshi era, when the Bright Hall and Circular Moat were built and the feudal lords held great court, he was summoned as grand herald; he fell ill, could not attend the guest ceremony, returned to his former post, and was dismissed on grounds of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He earned promotion to Chenliu by crushing the Jiangxia bandit Cheng Zhong; in Yuanshi, when the Bright Hall rose and lords gathered in court, he was called to be grand herald but took ill, missed the rites, went back to his old post, and retired sick.

sentences.zh: 復為中散大夫，終官。

translations.literal: Again he became grandee consultant and ended his career in office.
translations.idiomatic: He finished as a grandee consultant, still on the rolls when he died.

sentences.zh: 家至吏二千石者六七人。

translations.literal: In the family there were six or seven who reached two-thousand-bushel rank.
translations.idiomatic: Six or seven kinsmen reached two-thousand-bushel rank.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：蕭望之歷位將相，籍師傅之恩，可謂親暱亡間。

translations.literal: The appraising comment says: Xiao Wangzhi filled general and minister posts, relying on favor as tutor—he might be called intimate without breach.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: Wangzhi climbed to general and minister on the strength of the tutor's bond—close as family, it seemed.

sentences.zh: 及至謀洩隙開，讒邪構之，卒為便嬖宦豎所圖，哀哉！

translations.literal: When plans leaked and the rift opened, slanderers framed him, and in the end he was destroyed by favored eunuchs—alas!
translations.idiomatic: When secrets spread and the break widened, slanderers closed in, and palace favorites destroyed him—how pitiful.

sentences.zh: 不然，望之堂堂，折而不橈，身為儒宗，有輔佐之能，近古社稷臣也。

translations.literal: Had it not been so, Wangzhi, grand in bearing, broken but not bent, body Confucian patriarch, possessed the capacity to assist the altars of soil and grain—a minister for the altars in the near past.
translations.idiomatic: Otherwise this towering Confucian, broken yet unbowed, might have been the sort who steadies the dynasty itself.
